item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Bruh this is so clever, a favorite for sure.

item.title: One of the top two articles on a site dedicated to writing...
item.content:encoded:
…is a format screw designed to preclude writing. You have to love the irony.

I wonder if 2521 really is unable to identify infographic information about itself, or if it merely feels indifferent about visual forms of communication. I can imagine it impassively glancing over the infographic documentation while peering over the shoulder of a researcher, wondering, "Sheesh, why did they start communicating about me in such an unfun format? This doesn't spark joy. I'm not gonna bother bringing any more of these people back to my cool tree fort. And here I thought they were friendly! Lame."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love coming back to read— er, I mean view this one. It's a brilliant idea, communicating entirely in pictures. The testing logs somehow manage to be deeply unsettling, and the whole infographic leaves me desperately wanting to know more. This is one of my all-time favourite SCPs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is by far the easiest article to translate.
+

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: well that's kind of the question isn't it? We don't really know, and presumably the reason only 2 tests have been performed on 2521 is because it's so hard to communicate the intent to test it or any details of the test. You might be able to write something and have someone else read it REALLY quickly before 2521 takes it away, but that's iffy at best and it's not clear whether you'd be able to finish the document before the skip got there to take it

This was good enough to make me slightly paranoid that my computer is going to disappear after I post this, so +1
Only thing that would make it better would be if you could change the tags somehow so that they display as pictograms with link URLs to the actual tag page, but I don't know if the wiki software supports that

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This SCP got 4000 Upvotes this is 2/3 of SCP-173's upvotes

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really liked it! The Picturedetails are really realy cute, even if I needed some Minutes to understand it.

But I really want to know, what it does to you, if you say stuff about it.. I mean it seems to "love" Information about itself, so much so, it takes the Source with it.. But what about the People.. does it "Stash" them somewhere..kill them..integrade them ito itself..

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This SCP is really cool, despite the fact it might be hard to understand the document on first glance. This concept is amazing and pulled off in a great manner. Too bad I can only add just 1 upvote :(

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Disregarding the… questionable grammar (in my opinion, at least.), I do find what Sythe said to be somewhat interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I''m actually a little curious as to what this is. I might see if I can find any translators.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's Morse code, but they neglected to surround it with Wikidot's escaping syntax, so whenever there are two consecutive hyphens it causes a strikethrough. You could go through it, manually declutter it, and then translate it, but… eh.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't know what this means, and I don't know if I want to know what this means.

Actually, yeah. I want to know what this means.

Erm, how do you translate this? Whatever… this is?

item.title: Re: Screwy idea I had:
item.content:encoded: The same that would happen if John Wick and The Punisher joined forces.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
here ya go
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_3mu8hMzvuU

item.title: Screwy idea I had:
item.content:encoded: What would happen if you wrote "SCP-2521" on a photograph of SCP-096's face?

item.title: Re: Do I should get permission by the way?
item.content:encoded:
I want to make a crossover with the SCP I am gonna write. Anyone could draw it for me?

You want someone to make art of an SCP that doesn't exist yet? Because you're probably better off making the actual SCP and having it stay on the mainlist first. You know, so an artist can be sure that their work will actually be used?

item.title: Do I should get permission by the way?
item.content:encoded: I want to make a crossover with the SCP I am gonna write. Anyone could draw it for me?

item.title: Re: We failed...
item.content:encoded: My headcanon is that in-universe, those are auto-generated per article and that Tiberius ignores auto-generation. This also justifies the numbers in Di Molte Voci.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This deleted account was referring to this video, a successful attempt to do the impossible, namely narrate an unnarratable SCP. TheVolgun here refers to ●●|●●●●●|●●|● as Tiberius.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I just wanted to say this has always been my favourite SCP and I just got a tattoo so this little dude can be with me always

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Might I suggest that this be edited to include a form of the Anomaly Classification System? A version with only the hazard-diamondesque octagon could work around 2521’s properties.

Suggestion for classes:

Disruption Class: Ekhi1
Risk Class: Critical2

Footnotes
1. At worst. Depending on public knowledge of affected person(s), it could range from some cabin in the woods to entire countries or possibly the world. Possibly upgrade to Amida if things really go wrong.
2. Lost personnel are presumed to be killed.

item.title: Ikea
item.content:encoded: I’m building a multitude of furniture at the moment and I’ve suddenly been struck how similar this looks to the Ikea manuals! I’m getting spooked out building this shit like if I ask anyone verbally for help this SCP will come drag me to SCP-3008

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good imagination, this scp is perfect.

item.title: Re: Does anyone besides me think this is kind of boring?
item.content:encoded: Maybe it takes those with information to hoard the information about it self? Almost like a dragon with gold

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The foundation is called Secure, Contain, Protect for a reason. It doesn't SEEK to neutralize anything it doesn't have to.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Who the hell is Tiberius?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not for me

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Morse Code is a legitimate language with letters, ergo SCP-2521 might understand it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: His name translates to I 5 I E in morse code. It would be cool if his name was in actual morse code instead of adding dots.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I do appreciate some good vector art. And these are fantastic! Maybe in some future skips I'll have to try to incorporate some original pictographs or figures. Not that I'd copycat this idea, it's already perfect.

Wonderfully interesting, simple, an pretty.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Creatively genius.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One of the most unique SCPs on the wiki. Great idea, outstanding storytelling with the brilliant use of images instead of words.
Clearly worth a +1
Congrats on being the second page to hit 3k!

item.title: We failed...
item.content:encoded: I like it, an upvote, but if you scroll down far enough, it says SCP-2521 anyway, and hey, put that computer down!

item.title: Sign Language Workaround?
item.content:encoded: Would any of the many forms of Sign Language be a workaround for transmitting information about critter?

item.title: Re: Does anyone besides me think this is kind of boring?
item.content:encoded: The heart on its face seems to imply it enjoys being talked and written about, and anything bearing its name.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
*Inhales*

"SCP-2521"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This SCP is…. really sweet. It’s not malicious, it just wants a friend, you know? The stealing part is bad and so is the looking REALLY scary and moving through walls, but other than that I really like this one.

item.title: Re: ^~^
item.content:encoded: How is it Undertale-y? It's a monster, and that's the only thing it has in common with Undertale. Even then, Undertale didn't invent monsters.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is it just me, or does the skip shift around the images as you scroll?

item.title: Re: Does anyone besides me think this is kind of boring?
item.content:encoded: Eh, I think the heart means that he enjoys when people write/speak information about himself, and takes it because he likes it.
It’s cool and kinda undertale-y

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: legendary.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So here's a question that has been bugging me about this SCP.

So it's established that Tiberius will take any written information (plus the medium) and anyone that speaks about it.

But say, for example, someone drew information about Tiberius in the sand in a middle of a beach. What would he take then?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a great article and one of the only ones that can pull this off.

Australian -1'd.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Third highest rated SCP ever written yet people think it's gimmicky

Also, considering each one of those images had to be made, a lot of effort went into this

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This SCP is so creative and unique. I love the concept of it being aware of any obvious information about itself, and having to be described in the file with pictographs. Plus the Morse code nickname just makes it that much more unique. Amazing, one of my favorites.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm sorry, but I never understood why this won the short works contest, let alone become the 3rd highest ranking skip on the site. I always feel like there's something I'm missing that everyone else seems to get. It's a boring format screw, and not even a particularly imaginative one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think the lack of info pertaining to what happens to the information it steals is what makes it scary. It wouldn’t be as mysterious if we knew the true nature of the SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Separate letters and numbers don't matter, the creature doesn't hunt all information, only that pertaining to it. It can understand letters, but just a few random letters and numbers aren't enough to invoke it. Imagine this article was about you, but the browser didn't load the images- you could only see K here, an O there, some numbers. Would you understand it's about you? :)And about hieroglyphs - i believe any lettering system that can be spoken is enough to summon it. You can speak out the hieroglyphs, you can't "speak out" a picture, you can only describe it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
To this day one of the all-time best SCPs, and the one that really hooked me to this site. What people in the comments ignore in their focus on the creature itself, is the horrid implication of containment breach. There is a word - a name, a description out there… If you speak it out or try to pass it to another… "it" will come for you. It will dim the light, darken the day, pass through physical reality and manifest itself to you - huge, dark, and with long sticky tentacles, and only god knows where it will drag you to. It can not be stopped, it can not be reasoned with. And you will be forever gone, for daring to speak of the Unspeakable. Brilliant.
Some knowledge has the potential to wipe out humanity.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the creative way you portrayed the SCP. Using pictures instead of words was a pretty cool idea but we need more info on what happens after a person is “taken”. Other than that great job!

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…what's the length limit on uncollapsed text btw?

Depends on the screen, but if someone needs to scroll to read the entirety of the post without the screen zoomed in, usually that should be collapsed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just wondering - and forgive me if this has been touched on already in more than twenty pages of existing comments - but where does the dividing line come in between written and pictographic information? I mean… we have the letters A, B, D, i and K appearing quite plainly in the document, and each one has a particular commonly-understood meaning it conveys. Do they not count as words at all in this context?

Whilst the "i" may be difficult to replace (perhaps a "?" would do?), A and B could be replaced by a single and a double dot, the D maybe by four dots in a square (or nothing at all, as the subject's status is fairly obvious even without a letter), and the K is easily replaceable with a crown (as that's the literal translation of "Keter")…

edit: on backtracking, I realised there's also arabic numerals there, even though they were replaced with dots in the actual title. There's a red cross over people with 0, 1, 2 and 3 on them (which could be a bar, and then that number of dots), and a green circle over 4 and "O5" (the latter may be harder to replace; a downward-looking eye above five dots?). Plus there's the question over whether the red X's themselves may even count. A red "prohibition" sign (crossed-through circle) might work in place of the X, so long as it's not taken as a scandinavian letter (the bar not extending outside of the circle should be enough, or having it struck the opposite way), with the "good" case being a green tick/checkmark rather than a plain circle (or "O").

+ show block
– hide block

Of course, these might be deliberate mistakes showing that the foundation isn't entirely perfect with its drafting yet. Or that there's a certain amount of slop allowed and the being only responds to a certain density of written text. Plus of course none of them are actually directly concerning the being itself, other than maybe the K.

Also, I wonder if you could get away with using actual hieroglyphs. I mean, airline emergency procedure card style pictograms are rather more appropriate for a special containment procedure, as it needs to be quickly understood by as broad a spectrum of readers as possible, but if you wanted to encode more complex information that might be the way to go. Or indeed any form of non-alphabetic writing (ie, letterforms don't directly correspond to vocalised phonemes, but instead whole concepts; perhaps the direct correlation to speech is what matters? And maybe this scip has been around since before the development of writing…)

edit2: is there any significance to the tests being carried out in darkened rooms, with the only light being the desk lamp until after the test has finished? Does the being not appear in well-lit spaces? Maybe that's a question being left deliberately open also.

…what's the length limit on uncollapsed text btw? This doesn't feel over long to me, and well within the boundaries of what I'd comfortably read or indeed write on my phone.


As a courtesy to our readers on mobile devices, please collapse long posts. ~Zyn

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's a touch daft, but also cute.

…aaaand now that's somehow lead to me imagining it as an exiled character from Monsters Inc for some reason.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not much feeling beside "Great expression I'd say"

Then I saw the fan art from JP 静かな森
Didn't really try to find the original post till I saw the fan art

Tfw terrified setting turns into cozy anime in 3sec
